segments.text:
Do, Katoh 
Methods Mol 
Biol. 2008
Progressive Alignment steps:
1.
Perform pairwise alignments (similar to BLAST)
2.
Use results of pairwise alignment to build a distance matrix
3.
Use the distance matrix to build a guide tree
4.
Use the guide tree to perform the progressive alignment
a.
Align closely related sequences first
b.
Align groups of more distantly related sequences against each other
Bioinformatics Lecture 5: 
Multiple sequence alignments!
Recap…

Problems with progressive alignments:
1.
Not guaranteed to be “optimal”
I.
Not mathematically optimal – too many possibiliies!
II.
Not necessarily biologically correct
•
Best score may not correspond to the actual structural 
alignment
•
Machine will align any set of sequences you give it.  But there 
may be no actual alignment!
(Problems with any alignment approach)
2.
If guide tree is bad, wrong sequences will be aligned against 
each other initially
3.
Need good “dynamic range” of 
diversity to give a good alignment 
for full set of sequences
Result: Any aligment will have errors!
Our job:
• Identify problematic regions and/or sequences
• Figure out how to fix them as much as possible

How to identify poorly aligned sequences/regions?
1) See if there is anything obvious
•
If it looks like a frameshift….
• If a frameshift in the original: 
➞Delete or replace the sequence!

2) Look for patterns involving unusual or significant AAs….
C, F, Y, W.  Also G,P,  patterns of hydrophobic and charge.
Which sequence is misaligned?
Pays to get to know your sequence!
• If a region or a sequence is misaligned: 
➞Realign region OR realign sequence OR iterate alignment 
OR new program
- If a sequence is missing something that is everywhere else, 
it is either: 
a) misaligned
b) unrecognizable
c) missing that part
Realignment by hand: bad idea (!)

Not sure?
One useful approach: Dotplot!
Compare one sequence to another in sliding window
1. Matches
A match between sequences 
looks like a diagonal line on the 
dotplot graphic, representing the 
continuous match (or repeat).
2. Frame shifts
a. Mutations
Mutations are distinctions 
between sequences. On the 
graphic they are represented by 
gaps in diagonal lines. They 
interrupt matches.
b. Insertions
Insertions are parts of one 
sequence that are missed in the 
another, while the surrounding 
parts match. In other words, an 
insertion is a subsequence that 
was inserted into a sequence.
Graphically, insertions are 
represented by gaps which lie 
only on one axis. A little shift 
towards the other axis indicates 
a mutation involved.
c. Deletions
A deletion is a subsequence that 
was deleted from a sequence.
A deletion from sequence A 
found in sequence B can be 
considered as an insertion into 
sequence B and contained in 
sequence A.
Image and text: https://ugene.net/wiki/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=4227426

Dotplots: also good for finding repeats, low complexity regions
Dotlet: https://myhits.isb-
sib.ch/cgi-bin/dotlet
Compare human and 
mouse CLIP-170:
Use this one!

Can also BLAST two sequences (actually a global alignment)
Compare human and 
mouse CLIP-170:
Note series of other BLAST options.
Just scroll down the page!
- Works, but not set up to find repeats.

How to find conserved domains?
1. Graphic summary on BLAST: 
2. NCBI Conserved Domains Database (CDD)
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Structure/cdd/wrpsb.cgi or from BLAST: 
Note can ”search 
for similar domain 
architectures

Other options include:  http://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/
Interpro: includes coiled-coil, TM, disorder….
Probe 
CLIP-170
Note: bookmark results page – saved for 7 days

Assignment for Monday Sept. 16
Instructions
Please upload your homework by submitting to Sakai “Assignments” two files:
a) Your Clustal-generated ".aln" file that contains the alignment you made of your present set of sequences.
For most 
people, this will be the set of sequences you identified by PSI-BLAST in the model organism database, but for a few of you 
interested in non-human proteins, you might need to pull your proteins from a different database.
Whatever database you use, please don't try to submit here anything with more than 500 proteins - if your set is too large, 
please let me know ASAP, and we will do some work together to figure out how to whittle it down in a logical way.
b) A document (PPT or Word) that contains the following information:
i) The common name, accession number, and sequence of your probe protein (i.e., your protein of interest)
ii) Screenshots of what you find when you use your probe protein to search the NCBI Conserved Domain Database 
AND Interpro (see the class notes from Wednesday for links).
iii) A page (or so) that summarizes your analysis of your protein family as it now stands. Note that this will be 
unavoidably preliminary! The point is just to get you thinking about this.
For (iii), please include the following:
a) A summary of what domains exist in your protein, and why they might be functionally significant
b) An estimate of how many paralogs of your protein exist in the human genome (or your organism of primary 
interest). In making this estimate, please try to avoid including splice isoforms, and also avoid including proteins that 
simply have only a small part (e.g., one domain) in common with your protein. Note: for some protein families, this 
will be trivial, but for others, it will be quite challenging. Just give it a try, and if you have caveats, please list them.
c) An estimate of how far back in evolution your protein goes. In other words, in what range of organisms if your 
protein found? When and where did it appear? Again, for some proteins answering this will be trivial, but for others, 
it will be challenging because it is hard to tell what is the "same" protein in different organisms. To do this, one 
reasonable approach is to compare the domain organization of PSI-BLAST hits across different organisms. As with 
(b), just give it a try, and if you have caveats, please list them.
Please post to Piazza if you have problems!

The following information on coiled-coil searches, 
transmembrane searches, and disordered regions 
searches will be covered more on Monday, but some of 
you might want to include analyses of these domains in 
your homework.

How find domains not necessarily “conserved”?
Coiled-coil, Transmembrane, Disordered….
Typically need specific tools.
Suites of useful tools: • ExPASy – use “find resources”
• EBI (European Bioinformatics Institute)
Some more useful 
than others!
Most (?) useful for 
Coiled-coil: “Coils” (Lupas
algorithm)
https://embnet.vital-
it.ch/software/COILS_form.html

Transmembrane:
Most (?) useful (find from ExPASY or by search for these):
• Phobius http://phobius.sbc.su.se/
• TMHMM http://www.cbs.dtu.dk/services/TMHMM/
• TMPred (older and less definitive)

Disordered regions
List on Wikipdia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_disorder_prediction_software
• Disprot: database of experimentally characterized proteins
Most useful (?) Predictors based on sequence:
• IUPRED: https://iupred2a.elte.hu/
• PONDR: http://www.pondr.com/
Also: Interpro uses MobiDB, which presents a consensus 
answer for all UniProt entries using these 
tools: DisEMBL, ESpritz, GlobPlot, IUPred, Jronn, VSL2b.
